revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大臣家]][[ko:대신가]][[zh:大臣家]]
[[ja:大臣家]] '''Minister Families''' (daijinke) are one of the house ranks of {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|qid=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}}, ranking next after the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|qid=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}} and the {{ill|Seikake|ja|清華家|1=Seiga families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5367650}} in the classification of houses.
== Overview ==
The Minister Families originally emerged from cadet branches of the Seikake and advanced in rank corresponding to the Seikake (though, after serving as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}—a position not held by the Sesshō Houses and Seikake—they became {{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|qid=Q1185382|lt=Middle Counselor|1=Chūnagon}}). When a vacancy arose in the {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Major Counselor|1=Dainagon}}, they were promoted directly from Major Counselor to {{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Minister of the Center|1=Naidaijin}} without first serving as {{ill|Commander of the Imperial Guards|ja|近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11638972}}; unlike the Seikake, they could not concurrently hold the office of Commander of the Imperial Guards.
Concerning the distinction from the Seikake: originally, the Seikake were appointed to Councilor without serving as {{ill|Head of the Kurōdo|ja|蔵人頭|1=Kurōdonotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11621873}}, whereas the Minister Families served as Head of the Kurōdo before appointment as Councilor<ref>坂田桂一「中世前期における清華の家格とその昇進」(同志社大学文化学会『文化学年報』61、2012年)</ref>. However, some theories hold that over time the Seikake ceased to be appointed to Councilor<ref group="note">旧・七清華の当主は室町時代初期まで参議任官例があり、元和5年（1619年）に西園寺実晴と花山院定好が任ぜられたのが最後となっている。</ref> and that the Minister Families ceased to hold the office of Head of the Kurōdo<ref group="note">後奈良天皇以降の300年余りで大臣家から蔵人頭に任じられたのは正親町三条実有と中院通知の2名のみである。</ref><ref>林大樹「近世蔵人頭に関する基礎的考察」(國學院大学国史学会『国史学』217、2015年)</ref>.
Their highest official rank ({{ill|highest official rank|ja|極官|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 極位極官|1=highest official rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11541435}}) was the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm|1=Daijō-daijin}}<ref group="note">However, in the Edo period the office of Chancellor of the Realm was limited to those who had served as Sesshō or Kanpaku.</ref>, but there are rare examples of promotion to {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|qid=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}} (e.g., Sanjō Nariyuki; Nakoin Michitsuna), and in reality few instances exist of Minister Families advancing beyond the position of Minister of the Center.
Among those promoted to {{ill|Provisional Minister|ja|准大臣|1=jun-daijin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11395386}} or higher, the Nakoin family (25 heads from Michikata to Michitomi) produced ten individuals<ref group="note">18th head Nakoin Michitsuna was Minister of the Right; 2nd head to 15th head held Minister of the Center; 3rd and 9th heads held Provisional Minister.</ref>, the Sanjō Ōin family (29 heads from Sanjō Kimii to Ōin Kōshō) produced eight<ref group="note">5th–7th, 10th, 12th–13th heads held Minister of the Center; 9th and 15th heads received posthumous Provisional Minister.</ref>, and the Sanjō Nishi family (17 heads from Kimichika to Kōin) produced six<ref group="note">5th and 8th heads held Minister of the Right; others held Minister of the Center.</ref>. Thus, the rate of elevation to Daijin was not particularly high<ref>浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』中公文庫、90–91頁</ref>.
After the Meiji Restoration, based on the internal regulation of ennoblement under which there were many precedents of appointment up to Major Counselor, they were conferred the title of Count (hakushaku), but the Ōin family (Sanjō Ōin) was later elevated to Marquess (kōshaku) through promotion{{読み仮名|陞爵|しょうしゃく|qq=}}.
== List of Minister Families ==
The families belonging to the Minister Families, all old houses, are as follows:
=== Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in Branch (2 families) === * {{ill|Sanjō Ōin family|ja|正親町三条家|1=Ōgimachisanjō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545676}} (Ōin): a cadet branch of the Sanjō lineage of the Kan'in branch of the Fujiwara Hokke. Founded by Sanjō Sanefusa's son {{ill|Sanjō Kimii|ja|三条実房|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11355831}}. After the Meiji Restoration, renamed the Ōin family. Old house. Family occupations: court ceremonial rituals and classical etiquette (yusoku kojitsu). * {{ill|Sanjō Nishi family|ja|三条西家|1=Sanjōnishi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11355857}}: a cadet branch of the Ōin lineage of the Kan'in branch of the Fujiwara Hokke. Founded by Sanjō Jitsunaga's son {{ill|Sanjō Nishi Kimichika|ja|三条西公時|1=Kintoki Sanjonishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20040424}}. Old house. Family occupations: incense ceremony, waka poetry, and court ceremonial rituals.
=== Murakami Genji (1 family) === * {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|1=Nakanoin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11367812}}: a cadet branch of the Kuga lineage of the Murakami Genji. Founded by Kuga Michichika's son {{ill|Nakoin Michikata|ja|中院通方|1=Michikata Nakanoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11367816}}. Old house. Family occupations: court ceremonial rituals and classical etiquette.
== Notes == {{Notelist2|qq=}}
